title: Detection and quantification of extracellular microRNAs in murine biofluids
author: Roberts, Coenen-Stass, et al.
materials: i. Export RDML file from qPCR software.
iii. ‘Extract all’ from the new .zip file.
xiv. Click ‘OK’ to load the data.
xv. Click ‘determine baselines’ to initiate analysis.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: The biofluid RNA extraction protocol detailed below is scalable, such that extracellular RNA can be extracted from any volume of biofluid. 200 μl is a convenient volume as it enables the entire protocol to be performed in 1.7 ml microcentrifuge tubes.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: CAUTION: When working with RNA always use clean gloves, RNase-free plasticware and barrier pipette tips to prevent contamination of samples with RNases. Store RNA solutions on ice to minimize sample degradation.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 6) Aliquot a user-defined volume of biofluid into a clean RNase-free 1.7 ml microcentrifuge tube. We typically use 50 μl although the protocol can be easily scaled to accommodate larger volumes if required. (We have measured miRNA abundance from as little as 10 μl of biofluid). Using a constant volume of biofluid between samples is highly recommended so that samples prepared from different experiments can be directly compared.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 7) Adjust the volume of biofluid to 200 μl using nuclease-free water.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 8) Immediately add 600 μl of TRIzol LS reagent and mix by vortexing for 10 seconds.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 9) Add 3 μl of 5 nM external spike-in control to each sample and mix by vortexing for 10 seconds.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 10) OPTIONAL: Add 1 μl of 1 μg/μl MS2 Bacteriophage carrier RNA solution to improve extraction efficiency [41].

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 11) Incubate TRIzol LS/biofluid mixture for 5 minutes at room temperature.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 12) Add 160 μl of chloroform to each sample and mix by vortexing for 15 seconds.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 13) Incubate TRIzol LS/chloroform mixture for 15 minutes at room temperature.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 14) Centrifuge the samples at 12,000 g for 15 minutes at 4°C. After centrifugation the mixture separates into three phases. The clear aqueous top phase contains the RNA.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 15) Transfer the aqueous phase to a clean 1.7 ml microcentrifuge tube.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 16) OPTIONAL: Re-hydrate the phenolic phase by adding an additional volume of nuclease free water in order to maximize RNA recovery [5]. Vortex samples, repeat phase separation, pool the aqueous layers and proceed with isopropanol precipitation.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 17) CRITCAL STEP: Add 1 μl RNase-free glycogen to each sample to ensure efficient RNA extraction.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 18) Precipitate the RNA by adding 400 μl of isopropanol.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 20) Incubate samples for 10 minutes at room temperature.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: PAUSE POINT: Samples can be precipitated overnight if necessary.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 21) Centrifuge at 12,000 g for 10 minutes at 4°C. The RNA precipitate forms a gel like pellet at the bottom of the tube.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 23) Wash the RNA pellet with 1 ml of 75% ethanol.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 24) Vortex to resuspend the pellet and centrifuge at 7,500 g for 5 minutes at 4°C.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 26) Pulse-spin the samples using a bench-top microcentrifuge and remove the residual ethanol wash using a narrow pipette.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 27) Discard the supernatant and air-dry pellet for 10 minutes at room temperature.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: 28) Dissolve the RNA pellet in 30 μl RNase-free water and mix by pipetting. NOTE: It is recommended to always use the same volume of water when resuspending RNA pellets so that samples harvested from different experiments can be compared directly.

steps.title: RNA extraction from biofluids (TIMING: 3 hours)
steps.instruction: PAUSE POINT: Store samples at -80°C until ready for reverse transcription.

steps.title: Analysis of raw data (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: 43) Inspect data to ensure that all reactions show amplification curves as expected. (Sigmoidal or parabolic amplification curves indicate that fluorescence background is set incorrectly and can be adjusted manually).

steps.title: Analysis of raw data (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: 44) Check that negligible amplification is observed in negative controls (i.e. NTC and RT-).

steps.title: Analysis of raw data (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: 45) If intercalating dye methods (i.e. SYBR green) were used, check all reactions for expected melting profiles. Any reactions with multiple melt peaks or atypical melt profiles should be excluded from the analysis.

steps.title: Analysis of raw data (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: 46) Ensure that the y-axis for the amplification plot display is set to log scale and adjust the threshold value so that it is within the log linear phase of amplification across all reactions. (It is highly recommended that the threshold value for each assay be standardized across plates as this enables between plate comparisons to be made).

steps.title: Analysis of raw data (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: 47) If a standard curve was prepared, check that the estimated PCR efficiency is between 80% and 110%.

steps.title: Analysis of raw data (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: 48) If a standard curve was prepared, ensure that all samples fall within the linear range of the assay. Any samples that amplify outside of the range of the standard curve should be flagged as ‘Out-of-Range’. In particular, reactions that amplify after the lowest standard should be considered ‘Not Quantified’ or ‘Not Detected’ as appropriate.

steps.title: Analysis of raw data (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: 49) If a serial dilution series of template cDNA was used to prepare the standard curve, check that template inhibition does not occur in the high standard reactions. If template inhibition is observed, any samples that amplify in the problematic range should be excluded from the analysis.

steps.title: Analysis of raw data (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: 50) Export data from the analysis software to Microsoft Excel or an equivalent spreadsheet software package. Exported data include Cq values, amount in arbitrary units or amount in copies for relative quantification (Pfaffl analysis), relative standard curve and absolute standard curve analysis methods respectively.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: ii. Change file extension from .rdml to .zip (ensure windows is set to view file extensions).

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: iv. In extracted folder change the name of the file rdml_data.xml to my_experiment.xml.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: v. Re-zip the my_experiment.xml file to .zip format using the 7-zip software.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: ix. Select ‘Hydrolysis Probe’ in the ‘Monitoring Chemistry’ menu.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: x. Select ‘ss cDNA’ from the ‘Amplification of’ menu.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: xi. Select ‘cDNA sequence’ from the ‘Probe is targeting’ menu.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: xii. Select ‘No’ from the ‘Data are baseline corrected’ menu.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: xiii. Click on ‘Open RDML File’ button and browse to the location of the my_experiment.rdml file.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: xvi. When analysis is complete, click ‘File, Save to Excel’ (Microsoft Excel must be open).

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: xviii. The PCR efficiency for each individual reaction analysed is shown in column B of the Excel worksheet.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: xix. The mean PCR efficiency for the analysed plate is shown in column G of the Excel worksheet. These values are utilized in the Pfaffl analysis.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: i. Prepare a serial dilution (2-10 fold as required) of RT reaction product (from step 34).

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: ii. Perform qPCR as described in steps 35-42. Assign relevant reactions as standards in the qPCR software as appropriate.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: iii. An estimate of PCR efficiency is generally given automatically by the qPCR software. Alternatively, a standard curve can be drawn manually by plotting Cq values against log10(cDNA amount) for the serially diluted samples. PCR efficiency ‘E’ can be estimated from the gradient of the standard curve using Equation 3. A gradient of -3.32 corresponds with 100% PCR efficiency (E = 2).

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: i. Estimates of PCR efficiency are not required for absolute quantification.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: i. Pfaffl analysis is performed on Cq values exported from step 50 and requires PCR efficiency values determined in step 51. The Pfaffl equation is shown in Equation 1.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: ii. For each reaction, determine the amount of starting material by raising the value of the PCR efficiency to the power of –Cq.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: iii. Divide the gene-of-interest values by the reference values to obtain normalized values.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: iv. Scale the data by returning one of the experimental groups to a value of 1. i.e. divide each normalized value by the mean normalized value for the control group.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: i. Prepare a standard curve of synthetic RNA oligonucleotides as follows:

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: ii. Prepare 5 nM stocks of each synthetic oligonucleotide as described above (3.01 × 109 miRNA copies/μl).

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: iii. Add 3 μl of each 5 nM oligonucleotide to a fresh 1.7 ml microcentrifuge tube.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: iv. Make up to 1 ml using nuclease free water [or 20 ng/μl yeast tRNA solution as required] (9.03 × 106 miRNA copies/μl).

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: v. Prepare 10 fold dilution series (with 5 steps) using 20 ng/μl yeast tRNA solution as a diluent.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: vi. Use 5 μl of oligonucleotide/tRNA solution per 20 μl reverse transcriptase reaction.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: vii. Use 1.33 μl of cDNA per 20 μl qPCR reaction. (For the top standard, each qPCR reaction contains 3 million copies of synthetic target oligonucleotide).

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: viii. Reverse transcribe standards as described in steps 30-34 and perform qPCR as described in steps 35-42.

steps.title: Final data analysis (TIMING: <1 hour)
steps.instruction: ix. Copies per sample are determined by comparing samples against the standard curve.
